jokes.text: It's Wednesday, Homer. - [Screams] Work!
jokes.setup: Homer thinks it's Saturday and he's relaxing
jokes.punchline: Marge reveals it's Wednesday and Homer's horror at missing work
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer characterization - his complete obliviousness followed by panic. Great economy and structure.

jokes.text: I love these real Saturdays. They're so relaxing. Not like that fake Saturday that almost got me fired.
jokes.setup: Homer just panicked about missing work on Wednesday
jokes.punchline: He immediately refers to Wednesday as a 'fake Saturday' that almost got him fired
jokes.explanation: Brilliant immediate callback that shows Homer's twisted logic. 'Fake Saturday' is perfectly absurd Homer reasoning.

jokes.text: Oh, that. [Laughing] [Choking, Relieved Sigh]
jokes.setup: Marge discussing wasting one's whole life while Homer strangles Bart
jokes.punchline: Homer dismissively says 'Oh, that' about wasting life while continuing to choke Bart
jokes.explanation: Dark comedy juxtaposition - Homer's casual dismissal of life's meaning while literally choking his son.

jokes.text: Open a window. - [Groans]
jokes.setup: Marge complains about being cooped up in the house
jokes.punchline: Homer's literal interpretation - just open a window
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer obtuseness - missing the point entirely with overly literal solution.

jokes.text: We could find the drug boat of our dreams! I don't want a drug boat. Well, I bet there's drug dresses and drug vacuum cleaners too.
jokes.setup: Homer excited about police auction seized items
jokes.punchline: Homer assumes everything at auction is a 'drug [item]' including mundane household objects
jokes.explanation: Absurd Homer logic escalating to 'drug vacuum cleaners' - perfectly stupid and quotable.

jokes.text: Then what happened to Johnny D.? He forgot to lock 'em. Now, what am I bid? - One kilo. - Sold.
jokes.setup: Auctioneer selling bulletproof gates, someone asks what happened to Johnny D.
jokes.punchline: He forgot to lock them, then someone bids 'one kilo' (drugs) instead of dollars
jokes.explanation: Dark comedy about criminal incompetence plus bidding with drugs instead of money.

jokes.text: You bought a car without consulting me? I don't recall being consulted when you bought that hat. - I found this hat. - Then what are you complaining about? You got yours.
jokes.setup: Marge angry Homer bought car without consulting her
jokes.punchline: Homer's defense falls apart when Marge reveals she found her hat, then he says she still 'got hers'
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer logic failure - his argument collapses but he still claims victory. Great structure.

jokes.text: I'm not getting back in this car until you drive like a sane person. - [Tires Screeching] - Okay. Bye. Love ya.
jokes.setup: Marge demands Homer drive sanely or she won't get back in
jokes.punchline: Homer immediately peels out again while cheerfully saying goodbye
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer selfishness - completely ignores wife's safety concern while being casually affectionate.

jokes.text: You see, the law business is a little slow... and since most of my clients wind up losing their houses... this was a natural move for me.
jokes.setup: Hutz explaining his move from law to real estate
jokes.punchline: He caused his legal clients to lose their houses, so selling houses is a natural next step
jokes.explanation: Brilliant dark logic perfectly fitting Hutz's incompetent lawyer character. He's profiting from his own failures.

jokes.text: Yes, the money is good. But the beauty is you get to stay in the house until it's sold.
jokes.setup: Marge says helping people find homes must be rewarding
jokes.punchline: Hutz only cares about money and squatting in houses until they sell
jokes.explanation: Hutz's selfish corruption - he's essentially homeless and squatting. Classic Hutz sleaziness.

jokes.text: What is that, a six-barrel Holley carb? - You betcha. - Edelbrock intakes? - Nothir but. - Myohoff lifters? - Oh, yeah. - I made that last one up. - I see.
jokes.setup: Snake testing Homer's car knowledge with technical terms
jokes.punchline: Snake admits he made up the last term and Homer agreed anyway
jokes.explanation: Perfect setup exposing Homer's fake expertise. Snake's test and Homer's failure are beautifully executed.

jokes.text: Don't worry, Mom. We'll help you study. Of course, we'll have to cut back on our own homework.
jokes.setup: Lisa offering to help Marge study for real estate test
jokes.punchline: Lisa will neglect her own education to help her mother
jokes.explanation: Lisa's adult-like priorities and responsibility, ironically neglecting her actual kid duties.

jokes.text: So, you're married, and you're looking for your first house, Mr. and Mrs. - Superman. - Oh! Don't listen to my husband. He's just an idiot.
jokes.setup: Marge practicing real estate roleplay with Homer
jokes.punchline: Homer gives ridiculous fake name 'Superman' and Marge breaks character to insult him
jokes.explanation: Homer's childish fake name choice and Marge's exasperated honesty breaking roleplay.

jokes.text: I'm a veterinarian, and I need to keep lots of sick animals in my house. - Is that permitted? - Not in my damn house.
jokes.setup: Homer roleplaying as veterinarian wanting sick animals in house
jokes.punchline: Marge breaks character again to firmly reject this personally
jokes.explanation: Marge's protective instincts override her practice session. Good character consistency.

jokes.text: Well, this neighborhood is zoned R-3... which allows dogs, cats, phone-answering monkeys...
jokes.setup: Marge explaining zoning regulations
jokes.punchline: Phone-answering monkeys are apparently a normal zoning consideration
jokes.explanation: Absurd Springfield detail - phone-answering monkeys as routine animals. Good world-building comedy.

jokes.text: When I had to learn about the Magna Can'ta, I made up a song.
jokes.setup: Bart offering to help Marge memorize information
jokes.punchline: Bart's mispronunciation 'Magna Can'ta' instead of Magna Carta
jokes.explanation: Classic Bart ignorance - mispronouncing historical document. Shows his poor education amusingly.

jokes.text: You're all nuts.
jokes.setup: Family doing ridiculous educational song
jokes.punchline: Lisa's dry, adult assessment of the situation
jokes.explanation: Lisa as voice of reason cutting through family absurdity. Perfect economy and character voice.

jokes.text: Yar! I nailed that one about houseboats. Did you?
jokes.setup: Real estate exam just finished
jokes.punchline: Sea Captain obviously confident about houseboat question due to his nautical expertise
jokes.explanation: Perfect character-specific humor - Sea Captain would excel at boat-related real estate questions.

jokes.text: You didn't do anything. - I like being thanked.
jokes.setup: Marge thanking kids for help, Homer wanting thanks too
jokes.punchline: Homer admits he didn't help but still wants gratitude
jokes.explanation: Pure Homer logic - wanting credit without effort. Perfectly childish and honest.

jokes.text: And 42 of those years are Gill's! Marge, it's a real pleasure. You got any leads? I need some leads. Please, help me.
jokes.setup: Introducing experienced real estate agents
jokes.punchline: Gill immediately reveals his desperation despite decades of experience
jokes.explanation: Gill's pathetic desperation despite supposed expertise. Great character introduction showing failure.

jokes.text: Hi, Marge! Stay off the west side.
jokes.setup: Meeting Cookie Kwan, number one on west side
jokes.punchline: Immediate territorial threat delivered cheerfully
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy - immediate character establishment and threat. Became iconic one-liner.

jokes.text: A headset telephone? I thought those only existed in the movies.
jokes.setup: Marge seeing Nick Callahan with headset
jokes.punchline: Marge's innocent amazement at 1990s technology
jokes.explanation: Period-specific humor about technology. Shows Marge's sheltered perspective on business world.

jokes.text: I had been working on the Whitman place for 21 years.
jokes.setup: Nick just sold the Whitman place
jokes.punchline: Gill reveals he'd been trying to sell it for over two decades
jokes.explanation: Gill's spectacular incompetence - 21 years to fail at selling one house. Perfect loser character moment.

jokes.text: Are you guys talking about the west side? No, Cookie. I swear it. I'm scared of you.
jokes.setup: Other agents talking, Cookie appears
jokes.punchline: Agent's immediate fearful denial about discussing west side
jokes.explanation: Good callback showing Cookie's territorial intimidation works. Fear is immediate and honest.

jokes.text: Seat belts. [Scoffs] They kill more people than they save. That's not true. You're thinking of air bags.
jokes.setup: Bart asking about seat belts in the car
jokes.punchline: Homer's wrong safety claim corrected by Lisa with equally absurd claim
jokes.explanation: Homer's fake expertise met by Lisa's equally wrong 'correction' - both are spreading misinformation.

jokes.text: Gee, that picture makes your butt look big. I thought so too, but they said it sells. Works for the Lumber King.
jokes.setup: Looking at Marge's real estate billboard
jokes.punchline: Marge accepts that her big butt in the photo is good for sales, like Lumber King
jokes.explanation: Marge's practical acceptance of unflattering advertising and comparison to local business icon.

jokes.text: Lumber. We need lumber.
jokes.setup: Mentioning Lumber King billboard
jokes.punchline: Homer immediately convinced by advertising despite no actual need
jokes.explanation: Homer's susceptibility to advertising - perfectly in character impulse buying mentality.

jokes.text: What? No. Why? What? Why? What have you heard? Are you implying I'm in some sort of financial trouble? - No. - Well, I am. Let me have that card.
jokes.setup: Marge asking if Moe considered selling his home
jokes.punchline: Moe's paranoid escalation ending in admission of financial trouble
jokes.explanation: Perfect Moe paranoia and self-sabotage. Great escalating structure from defensive to desperate.

jokes.text: Why, isn't this kitchen horribly cramped? [Laughing] Why, yes, it is. Well, I suppose we could get used to it. Yeah, but you shouldn't have to settle.
jokes.setup: Client pointing out kitchen flaws
jokes.punchline: Marge agrees instead of spinning it positively, then talks them out of settling
jokes.explanation: Marge's honesty sabotaging the sale - she can't help but be truthful and helpful.

jokes.text: If you ever need a prescription, no questions asked- [Clicks Tongue, Laughing]
jokes.setup: Client thanking Marge for honesty
jokes.punchline: Client offers illegal prescription drugs as payment/favor
jokes.explanation: Dark humor about corrupt doctors offering illegal prescriptions casually.

jokes.text: Actually, this is our first time. And I didn't really care for it. Oh, well, then you have to ask yourself, is this the right house for you?
jokes.setup: Couple looking at house with bowling alley
jokes.punchline: Marge talks them out of buying when they admit they don't like bowling
jokes.explanation: Marge again sabotaging her own sale through honesty and genuine concern for clients.

jokes.text: My high school sweetheart was killed in a drag racing accident. - Come on. It'll be fun. - That's what Debbie Sue said.
jokes.setup: Homer asking Skinner to drag race
jokes.punchline: Skinner's tragic backstory and implication that Debbie Sue said racing would be fun before dying
jokes.explanation: Dark humor with perfect Skinner backstory. 'That's what Debbie Sue said' is brilliantly tragic and quotable.

jokes.text: Hey, that smells like regular. She needs premium, dude! Premium, dude!
jokes.setup: Snake escaping to save his car from mistreatment
jokes.punchline: Snake's emotional plea about proper gasoline grade for his car
jokes.explanation: Snake's passionate care for his car's needs. 'Premium, dude!' became a quotable moment.

jokes.text: The right house is the house that's for sale. The right person is anyone.
jokes.setup: Hutz teaching Marge real estate philosophy
jokes.punchline: Cynical reduction of real estate to pure sales without regard for client needs
jokes.explanation: Perfect Hutz cynicism - reducing human needs to sales opportunities. Excellent structure and quotability.

jokes.text: I'd say it's awfully cozy. - [Marge] That's dilapidated. - Rustic. - That house is on fire. - Motivated seller.
jokes.setup: Hutz teaching Marge positive real estate spin
jokes.punchline: Escalates to calling a burning house a 'motivated seller'
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalation with perfect structure. 'Motivated seller' for burning house is comedy gold - highly quotable.

jokes.text: But you'd better sell something. Because cubicles are for closers, Marge.
jokes.setup: Hutz explaining sales pressure
jokes.punchline: Reference to 'Glengarry Glen Ross' - 'coffee is for closers'
jokes.explanation: Good movie reference adaptation. Works for character and situation, though relies on external reference.

jokes.text: As a spineless, potato-cooking housewife who can't compete in the real world? [All] Mm-hmm. Well, I can too compete.
jokes.setup: Marge asking if family sees her as spineless housewife
jokes.punchline: Family's unanimous agreement followed by Marge's determined response
jokes.explanation: Great family dynamic moment - their honest agreement motivating Marge. Good character development.

jokes.text: Please don't tell anyone how I live.
jokes.setup: Looking at extremely shabby house
jokes.punchline: Gill's embarrassed admission that this hovel is his home
jokes.explanation: Perfect Gill pathetic moment - professional real estate agent living in squalor. Great character consistency.

jokes.text: It's sure built solid. The kids could scream bloody murder, and no one would hear.
jokes.setup: Ned praising house construction quality
jokes.punchline: Inadvertently describing perfect murder house features
jokes.explanation: Dark ironic humor - innocent Ned unknowingly describing murder house benefits. Excellent dramatic irony.

jokes.text: I'm just gonna spill my guts. I love it to death.
jokes.setup: Ned enthusiastic about the murder house
jokes.punchline: Using death metaphors while buying house where people were murdered
jokes.explanation: More dark irony with Ned's innocent death metaphors. 'Spill my guts' and 'love to death' in murder house.

jokes.text: That's good enough for me! [Screams] Purple drapes! All my life I've wanted purple drapes! [Screams]
jokes.setup: Ned about to buy the house
jokes.punchline: Ned's bizarrely intense excitement about purple drapes specifically
jokes.explanation: Absurd Ned obsession with specific decorative detail. His screaming excitement is perfectly over-the-top.

jokes.text: Boo-yah! Wow, you must've told a whopper to unload that death trap.
jokes.setup: Marge sold the murder house
jokes.punchline: Nick assuming she must have lied extensively to sell the notorious house
jokes.explanation: Nick's cynical assumption about sales tactics. 'Death trap' is appropriately direct for murder house.

jokes.text: What'd you use, Marge? The old buff and bluff? The Hail Murray? The Susquehanna Shuffle?
jokes.setup: Agent asking about Marge's sales technique
jokes.punchline: Ridiculous made-up names for sales tactics, especially 'Susquehanna Shuffle'
jokes.explanation: Creative absurd sales tactic names. 'Susquehanna Shuffle' stands out as particularly silly and quotable.

jokes.text: Just because everyone who lived in that house was hacked to bits... doesn't mean the Flanders will be.
jokes.setup: Discussion about murder house sale
jokes.punchline: Casually mentioning gruesome murders while reassuring about Flanders' safety
jokes.explanation: Dark humor treating gruesome murders casually. Good contrast between horrific detail and optimistic outlook.

jokes.text: Baldy boy hits wire, head comes off... L'il Bandit rolls to a gentle stop. Everybody wins.
jokes.setup: Homer planning to decapitate someone with wire trap
jokes.punchline: Homer's cheerful description of planned murder as 'everybody wins'
jokes.explanation: Homer's sociopathic logic - planning murder for car recovery as win-win scenario. Darkly funny.

jokes.text: Now that you mention it, we could use- - It was just an expression.
jokes.setup: Homer offering help to moving Flanders
jokes.punchline: Homer immediately withdraws offer when Ned tries to accept
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer selfishness - offering help he doesn't want to give, then backtracking immediately.

jokes.text: we just wanna get to our new house and rest in peace.
jokes.setup: Ned talking about moving to new house
jokes.punchline: Using 'rest in peace' phrase about moving to murder house
jokes.explanation: Dark dramatic irony - innocent Ned using death phrase about murder house. Excellent ominous foreshadowing.

jokes.text: This is my car! And I'm not fat! It's glandular!
jokes.setup: Snake calling Homer fat while fighting over car
jokes.punchline: Homer's defensive claim about glandular weight condition
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer defensiveness about his weight with medical excuse. Predictable but in-character.

jokes.text: We were painting Todd's room red- pretty messy work- and then we came out here to take a break.
jokes.setup: Flanders covered in red, looking like murder victims
jokes.punchline: Innocent explanation - they were painting Todd's room red
jokes.explanation: Perfect misdirection - audience thinks they're murder victims, but it's innocent paint. Great visual gag setup.

jokes.text: This is Wiggum reporting a 318- waking a police officer.
jokes.setup: Wiggum reporting incident during car chase
jokes.punchline: He reports his own violation - being woken up while on duty
jokes.explanation: Perfect Wiggum incompetence - reporting himself for sleeping on duty during active chase. Great self-incrimination.

jokes.text: And this is the butler's pantry, where Mrs. Astor concealed herself. And right here's where they found the torso heap- in front of our very own fireplace!
jokes.setup: Ned giving tour of murder house history
jokes.punchline: Ned's enthusiastic description of gruesome murder details as home features
jokes.explanation: Dark comedy gold - innocent Ned treating murder scenes as charming historical features. 'Torso heap' by fireplace.

jokes.text: Look, Daddy. I'm the jealous jockey. - I'm a torso.
jokes.setup: Flanders kids playing in murder house
jokes.punchline: Kids innocently roleplaying the gruesome murders
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly dark - innocent children playing murder victim roles. 'I'm a torso' is perfectly disturbing and hilarious.

jokes.text: Uh, Marge- [Chuckles] you still got that deposit check?
jokes.setup: House crashes down around them
jokes.punchline: Ned's immediate concern about getting money back
jokes.explanation: Perfect timing - house collapses and Ned immediately thinks about deposit. Good priorities reversal.

jokes.text: Lucky thing we landed on this bubble wrap. - [Popping] - Hey, quit hoggir. - Oh, I'm badly hurt.
jokes.setup: Everyone crashes and lands on something soft
jokes.punchline: They're fighting over bubble wrap while claiming to be badly hurt
jokes.explanation: Physical comedy with competing claims of injury while fighting over comfort. Decent character interaction.

jokes.text: [Laughing] You're one of a kind, Marge... and nobody deserves this more than you.
jokes.setup: Hutz firing Marge for being too honest
jokes.punchline: Hutz sarcastically saying she 'deserves' being fired for her integrity
jokes.explanation: Hutz's twisted logic - firing someone for good qualities while claiming they deserve it. Good character consistency.

jokes.text: $300 for doing nothing? I feel like such a crook. Don't worry. Gets easier every week.
jokes.setup: Marge feeling guilty about unemployment benefits
jokes.punchline: Patty's cynical encouragement about accepting unearned money
jokes.explanation: Good contrast between Marge's guilt and Patty's cynicism about welfare system. Character-appropriate attitudes.
